dialogue.utterance: Dispatch Unit 3 for an overdose behind a house in East End.
Hi, I'm Niko, an EMT on the rescue team. What happened?
Man found unresponsive behind the house with agonal breathing.
Patient is unresponsive to pain. Any eye opening or vocal response?
No eye opening. No movement with painful stimuli applied.
No motor response expected with GCS of 3.
No signs of airway obstruction today. Clear airway.
Breathing is agonal, approximately 1 per minute. No chest rise noted.
Pulse is 125 and strong. Skin is cyanotic, cool, and clammy.
Friend says he saw the man go into the house, and later he was found unresponsive.
Friend mentions past prescriptions but can't confirm current meds. He notes Seroquel, Xanax, and Wellbutrin.
Skin is cyanotic with no rashes or bites. Cool, clammy.
Pupils are pinpoint and equal. No trauma to head or neck noted.
Left chest has a tennis ball-sized bruise. No chest rise with respiration.
Pulse is 125, BP is 155 over 84. Getting the SpO2 and EKG now.
Breathing is 1 per minute. No change in pattern observed.
SpO2 is 98%. EKG shows sinus tachycardia.
Unmeasurable at this time-he's unresponsive.
Blood pressure is regained at 155/84. Let's proceed with treatment.
Begin BVM assisted ventilation. Lift is secured.
Apply 4-lead EKG monitoring now as backup.
Administering 2 mg of Narcan intranasally now.
Pulse is 113, BP is 157/92, and SpO2 is 97%. Minimal improvement noted.
Applying physical restraints for transport safety.
Inserting nasopharyngeal airway. No resistance found.
Second dose of Narcan given. Patient's eyes open to pain.
Following protocol 7-1 for opioid overdose-airway, BVM, and Narcan.
Transporting to Chippenham per protocol. Radioing ahead.
RFD and EMS crews transferred the patient securely to the stretcher.
Additional dose administered. More eye response and slight improvement in breathing.
Patient is secure and restrained in the unit. No movement.
Arrived at the hospital. Patient is now A&Ox1 to 4.
Respiratory rate improved to about 10 per minute.
Eyes are reacting better, and SpO2 has improved to 97%. No pain response noted.
Suspected fentanyl ingestion based on friend's statements.
Known psychiatric conditions: schizoaffective disorder and depression history.
All steps of protocol 7-1 completed. Positive effect noted per standing order.
Patient transferred to ER with assistance. A&Ox4 now in the ER.
Hospital accepted the patient. Trending improvement reported.
No complications occurred during transfer and handover. All steps followed.
Breathing adequate at 10 per minute. Vitals stable on hospital triage report.
Protocol fully followed. EKG, BVM, Narcan, and rest on scene.
Final handoff completed. Patient is now responsive and with further care in ER.
Patient accepted by hospital properly without incident.
No field glucose measurement. Documented as undetectable.
No drug allergies reported. No visible trauma observed.
Perfusion with cap refill as expected. Peripheral pulses present.
Protocol successfully completed based on positive response to Narcan.
No definitive substance determined but fentanyl strongly suspected.
Patient handed off and no EMS services needed post-entrance.
Scene left with no changes in status. All procedures documented.
Patient remains in acceptable condition for transport.
Patient was picked up from the ground. Friend noted intent to ingest fentanyl.
No abdomen exam was performed. Patient did not move extremities.
No nasal or oral trauma identified. No oral drainage.
Vital signs were stable despite initial critical condition.
SpO2 increased to 97%. Performance of interventions confirmed in stabilization.
Persistent sinus tachycardia through EKG monitoring. No arrhythmias noted.
Airways remain clear. No intervention required for airway clearance at this time.
BP remains stable at 157/92 during transit. Trend downward from 125 initial.
Only one patient found. All procedures followed without challenge or change.
Declared transported stable and accepted to Chippenham hospital safely.
A&Ox4 now with minimal aid. No need to remove restraints.
Patient drug reversal achieved. Naloxone effectiveness confirmed.
No original complaints except unresponsiveness. No complaints required in transport.
Mental health conditions documented per friend's recall and pre-hospital care.
EKG monitoring continued. No changes in rhythm observed during transport.
No transfer procedures documented at this point. No incident recorded.
Blood pressure still stable throughout emergency room handoff process.
No medication side effects documented. No adverse signs observed.
Oxygen saturation sustained at 97% after initial improvement.
No requirement for repeat intervention. Patient responded well to current protocol.
All transfers went seamlessly. No EC interventions required.
Patient able to move and coordinate necessary transfers on command.
All team members coordinated to prevent complications and ensure patient safety.
Last dose of Narcan administered in ambulance. Full response confirmed on ED entry.
Patient remained in conscious status with no incidence from transport.
No additional constraints applied while in the ED. Patient followed all commands.
No field interventions were needed after hospital turn over.
All standing orders were about treatment, transport, and hospital transition.
Patient cooperative upon arrival in Chinese Medical Center through HEAR radio.
EMS team provided timely and accurate implementation of the standing order.
ER nurse was ready for patient arrival. No complications reported in discharge.
Request for further documentation between ER and EMS complete. No inconsistencies.
Diagnosed with major depression and schizoaffective disorder-recorded pre-transfer.
Blood sugar measurements in community field remain undetectable.
No requirement for post-hospital care. Patient per hospital to follow up.
No discrepancies in transport. All dose, time, and care followed as-specified.
Patient carried to recovery unconsciously until Narcan interventions instructed.
EMS handover recorded in a high-quality manner for the ER team by EMS crew.
EMS team returned to call station after final transport obligation met.
All team members accounted and returned safely, no incidents.
Scene left clean and ordered with no evidence or patient belongings remaining.
No issues reported by the hospital or fire department. All data matches ePCR record.
Time-synchronized procedures documented under protocol binding for charging.
No unaccounted deviations between field report and hospital log.
Entry, movement, and exit protocols reviewed without misstep.
Patient outcome shows all interventions necessary for survival and comfort following overdose.
All transition procedures done in an organized and efficient fashion.
EMS service completed standards-based care response and handoff.
